text: Hi friends, this video is about an elderly gentleman who had an advanced metastatic gastric carcinoma with gastric outlet obstruction.
The video shows a tumor involving the anthropyloric region of the stomach, with visible tissue changes and possible obstruction in the area.
The surgeon mobilizes the omentum, visually inspecting the area to locate the TJ flexure.
The surgeon pushes the omentum cranially to expose the duodenum and locate the duodenal jejunal flexure.

The surgeon identifies the starting point of the duodenojejunal (DJ) flexure, noting the fixed portion and its attachment at the root of the mesentery. The measurement of 30 to 40 centimeters is taken to determine the optimal jejunal limb for reaching the stomach without tension.

The surgeon measures the jejunal loop, ensuring the biliary pancreatic limb is positioned to the right and the alimentary limb to the left. The small bowel is visible as the surgeon checks for adequate length and tension before bringing the loop towards the stomach.

The surgeon places a stay suture on the jejunum and stomach, approximately seven to eight centimeters above the planned site for enterotomy and gastrotomy, ensuring the loop remains in position without tension.

The surgeon uses a long PD suture to place a stay suture, securing the stomach and jejunum in close proximity. A small square knot is tied to maintain the position of the limbs, with a couple of bites sufficient for this temporary hold.
The surgeon positions the stomach and jejunum, then creates a small enterotomy in the jejunal loop using a monopolar hook, preparing for the anastomosis.
The surgeon enters the jejunum and gently pulls the mucosa, dividing it to facilitate the anastomosis.
The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to carefully enlarge the entrotomy in the jejunum, ensuring the opening is sufficient for the upcoming anastomosis.
The surgeon selects the gastrotomy site and uses a monopolar hook to create the gastrotomy, ensuring minimal bleeding during the incision.

The surgeon carefully enters the stomach, creating a submucosal tunnel. Using a Maryland dissector, the gastrotomy is opened wider than the entrotomy, ensuring clear visual access to the stomach contents. Excess mucosa is cauterized or cut with scissors to achieve a sufficient opening.
The surgeon uses suction to remove the contents from the jejunum, which are visible as they are evacuated from the surgical field.
The surgeon inserts the large jaw of the stapler into the stomach through the enlarged gastrotomy, which is visibly larger than the entrotomy.

The surgeon carefully manipulates the stomach and small bowel, ensuring the small jaw of the stapler is introduced into the small bowel without pushing, to avoid perforation. The small bowel opening is kept minimal despite elongation from manipulation and traction.
The surgeon uses a 60mm stapler to join the stomach and small bowel, ensuring precise alignment of the gastrotomy and entrotomy sites.
Anything close to forty or forty five is good enough. So here deliberately we are using a longer one so that the patient will not have too much of a trouble with regards to stasis.
The surgeon carefully removes the stapler, ensuring the jaws remain closed. The staple line is examined for any signs of bleeding along the tissue.

The surgeon secures hemostasis at the inferior aspect of the anastomosis by placing a suture. The first knot is tied with three to four throws, and the suture is left slightly longer after being divided.
and I'll show you a little later in the video how this is going to be useful for us when we come towards the later part of the anastomosis.
The surgeon performs squared knots to secure the sutures, ensuring stability and minimizing the risk of loosening during the jejunostomy procedure.

The surgeon uses a monofilament suture to take a surgeon's knot followed by three single throws, then divides the thread. The suture is placed at the superior aspect of the anastomosis, taking a bite through the thick submucosa and minimal mucosa.

The surgeon begins suturing approximately half to one centimeter above the gastrotomy and entrotomy sites, in an area where stapling has already occurred, carefully knotting the suture to secure the tissue.
The surgeon performs three to four throws of simple squared knots to secure the suture, ensuring the knots are tight and stable.
The surgeon completes several knots, reducing the length of the suture thread, and begins continuous suturing. The thick submucosa is visible, with minimal mucosa included in the suturing process.

The surgeon takes controlled bites on both sides of the stomach using a monofilament suture, ensuring standard traction. The needle passes through a small portion of the mucosa and a substantial bite of the submucosa, demonstrating the advantage of monofilament in maintaining tension without continuous traction.

The surgeon takes a controlled bite on the stomach side, with the assisting hand holding the needle for retrieval. The needle is loaded directly, and the thread is pulled efficiently. A good bite is taken on the thick submucosa with minimal mucosa involvement.

The surgeon takes a controlled bite on the stomach side, precisely guiding the needle to exit at the intended area. The needle is held securely, and the thread is pulled through to continue the suturing process.

The surgeon uses an instrument at the enterotomy site to visualize both lips separately, ensuring precise suturing without accidentally piercing the posterior wall. After two to three bites, the thread effectively pulls the tissues closer together with ease.

The surgeon removes the RALS tube before introducing the stapler. After firing the stapler, the RALS tube is advanced and kept in place. The anastomosis is visually inspected, showing well-inverted tissues without aversion. The first suture placed on the inferior aspect is pulled to better identify the tissue.

The surgeon demonstrates the advantage of pulling the thread from the first suture placed on the inferior aspect, allowing for a secure bite at the desired point and ensuring proper tissue alignment during the jejunostomy procedure.
The surgeon completes the anastomosis or enterotomy closure by pulling the thread to bring both tissue edges outside, demonstrating controlled manipulation of the tissues.

The surgeon identifies the edges of the small bowel and stomach, taking a thick, secure bite to complete the anastomosis. The needle holder is positioned close to the tissue as the thread is tightened in a controlled manner, preparing for the final knotting.
The surgeon stabilizes the tissues and tightens the thread, completing the anastomosis. The curvature of the needle is used to perform knotting, ensuring secure closure of the anastomosis site.
The surgeon holds and tightens the thread, demonstrating the perfect length of the suture. The procedure utilizes a single PDS suture without the need for additional sutures.
The surgeon uses the remnant thread from the jejunostomy procedure to close the surgical ports and the skin incisions where the stapler and camera were introduced.
The surgeon uses the curvature of the needle to perform knotting and suturing comfortably, demonstrating the technique as the laparoscopic gastrojejunostomy procedure concludes.
I'd be happy to take any of your doubts. Thank you very much.
